# Transcrição: 1.  The Attribution Trinity Explained

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Now that we've discussed the tracking trinity and you understand the importance of making sure you've got data, the next part is making sure you're making the right decisions. This is the attribution part of the FAT framework. This relies on you having the right data in place. So first of all, on the right hand side, it says, what does Facebook say? In order for that to be accurate or as accurate as you can make it, you need your browser pixel working properly. You need your conversion API, pixel, let's call it, working properly. And if you can, you need to get your offline conversion data in as well. So that gives you the best chance of figuring out what does Facebook say and can I rely on it? Then the second part is Google Analytics. Now you could replace this for other tracking platforms. The reason I chose GA to illustrate and the reason I use GA is, first of all, it's free and secondly, it's amazing. I don't know if people really, really understand the power of Google Analytics. I've been using it since 2005, give or take. And in that time, it's remained with me. It doesn't matter what I've been advertising as an affiliate in e-com, in info products, all that kind of stuff. GA has always been there for me and it's adapted a lot and it's evolved. And even then, I still feel I don't really use it to anywhere near 100% of its potential. However, for this training and the subsequent videos, you'll see how powerful GA can be. And I would probably hesitate and say, I've probably gone into about 5% of GA's capability but by the time you've seen how we're using the framework, it should wet your appetite and also make you realize how much more you can do with it as well. And that again relies on having the data set up properly, which means the Google Analytics Pixel is set up properly, making sure that things are tracking. You've got e-commerce tracking, if you're running e-commerce for example, and that you can use the right tracking parameters, sending traffic into GA. So there's lots of stuff to get in place there, which I'll show you in that part of the training. Then the third part is, what does Shopify say? So this training was specifically created thinking about running ads into a Shopify store. However, that's really because of the way Shopify developed their tracking to use Google Analytics data. And I'll show you that as well. You could replace this for any other platform. So Shopify is what I use to illustrate the examples here. If you're using another platform and they're able to give you data on your marketing, then you can also use that. The data going into these three areas are also based on how they're reporting is set up. So for Facebook on the right here, this is only Facebook data. So Facebook app, which is Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, etc.

## [03:02 - 06:04]

So what Facebook are looking at is that when the pixel is installed on your site or your server, Facebook can see all the data coming through. But for reporting purposes, it's just looking at click data and then maybe view data that's been generated from Facebook. So in that sense, it's a bit narrow minded. It's not really thinking or worrying about all these other platforms that you might be using. They have the data and as part of the attribution team at Facebook, they were doing some really good stuff prior to the example changes. That's one limited view of the data. And if that's all you use for optimizations, you're probably missing out on 10, 20% opportunity anyway from looking at other data sources. When you look at Google Analytics, it's not necessarily better. It's a different view. Now with Google Analytics, when you look at conversions, for example, so if you look at your e-commerce report and you look at how many purchases were made, Google Analytics generally shows you the last click or the last interaction that someone's had. So for example, if all you do is run Facebook ads, but you also have email that collects abandoned carts and gets them back in. And let's say abandoned carts drives 40% of your revenue, then if you're looking GA, then it will show that 40% of your revenue came through email. Yet you might look at that and say, well, I spend 100% of my ad money on Facebook. I know that Facebook's generated that money. And this is where some of the confusion lay and this is where some of the optimization opportunities lay because the right answer isn't to say, hey, I'm spending all this money on Facebook so Facebook should get all the attribution. This is talking about how to make your channels work effectively together. And one of the videos later in the training talks about how to understand attribution. I use the medium of football or soccer for the North Americans to explain how attribution works and why it's so important to get it right because that's the key to be able to scale successfully. This isn't a case of I want to spend as much money on Facebook as possible and I'm going to narrow mindedly just focus on this one platform and I want this platform to be the best and the only one that I use. That's not really how companies grow. So a big part of this kind of training is for you to open your eyes to that. If they're not already opened or if they are and you're aware, then getting you to understand how I do that and how I bring those different data points together. Then the final part is using Shopify. And one of the cool things about this is Shopify right now enables you to look at GA tracking parameters so you can actually see what's happening in your back end. So your Shopify data can be broken out in the Shopify platform. Now bear in mind Shopify is not an analytics tool. They are trying to add some analytics. There is only one true analytics tool here of the three and you can probably guess which one it is. It's Google Analytics. Like it's Google's day job. It's Google Analytics' day job to look at your data. Look are really there to make money off you.

## [06:04 - 08:03]

So as far as they're concerned, they want to show that your ads are working really well so that you spend more money. That's the bottom line motivation. What's Google's motivation for you to be logged into Google Analytics is to give you the best data so you can make quick decisions, your business grows, etc. And then what they hope is that you upgrade to their paid platforms. What's Shopify's goal? Similar to Facebook. They want you to stand on Shopify and show you that Shopify is great, etc. So for me, Google Analytics, I build my source of truth as much as I can around that. But then I also know that Shopify also holds 100% of your sales data. So you kind of need that. So the attribution trinity is about bringing these three together to make decisions so that when you're looking at data, you're able to feed that into your advertising based on as many facts as you can gather. So if you go back to 2020, 2019, etc., you could very easily scale a Facebook ad account by just looking at Facebook, even though it's not the right thing to do. The majority of people out there did it. The dropshippers, the affiliates, and so many people that have huge case studies. But now they're all struggling because Facebook is not showing them the right data. It's not showing them breakdowns and things like that. And that's where it's so important to look at other data sources. And you can replace this for TikTok, Snapchat, any other ad platform. Whatever it is, at the very minimum, you should have your analytics platform to look at data. So even if you don't have a back end platform that you can rely on, let's say for example you're running into a platform that doesn't show you any marketing stats, but you've got your sales data in there, you can still use your ad platform and your analytics. And that's the real key essential, I believe, to making your advertising work is to make sure that you've got your data set up properly and it's reliable. And you're using that to make key decisions.
